author: Dat dsgnr
body:
"https://rollmaball.figma.site/  

What if your Figma file wasn't the mockup, but the actual game? This game's honestly just an excuse. The real thing I wanted to prove is that Figma can be the actual live engine and database behind an app, not just where you draw the mockup. So I built a multiplayer voxel world à la Minecraft, with rolling balls, where the blocks, textures, and physics all live as Figma components. Edit a layer in the file and the world changes live. No redeploy, no export. You can (after a small cleanup in the coming days) even fork my Figma file, mod your own world in it, an"

author: Kylie Madsen
body: "The worst part of getting interrupted isn't the time you lose. it's losing your place and the climb back in. We built Re-Entry : capture the thread, take a bounded reset, redock to the exact task in seconds. a productivity tool that wins by getting you out fast. made with figma. #ConfigMakeathon"

author: Shweta D
body: "what started as conversations about terrible situationships turned into a working product.  while dating, details get forgotten and the same mistakes keep repeating themselves. Personally I do know how easy it is to rewrite history when feelings are involved. A red flag becomes ""maybe I was overthinking."" A mixed signal becomes ""they probably meant something else."" Anyone who has been through a bad breakup or the uncertainty of a situationship, probably knows exactly what I'm talking about.  @rey that idea became LORE 🔥  LORE is a private tracker for connections, situationships and relationsh"

author: Vajahat Ali
body:
"I have created ' TrendVerse '. A webApp as a fun and interactive dashboard without looking like a dashboard. 😊 TrendVerse
A living space for culture intelligence — built in Figma Make The Problem Culture moves fast. Brand strategists, creators, and marketers open Google Trends, Exploding Topics, TikTok Creative Center, and Reddit — every single morning — and still feel behind. Not because the data is missing. Because every existing tool shows you culture as a spreadsheet. Static. Cold. Disconnected from how trends actually feel. The Insight There's a specific anxiety people carry every day — "

author: Gonçalo Medeiros
body: "Cities run on 311 hotlines and email forms. Issues disappear. Citizens never find out what happened. muni is public issue tracking for modern municipalities, built for the way people actually live. Citizens can report problems, track resolution in real time, and upvote what matters most so local governments work on what's actually urgent. AI auto-categorizes issues and blocks duplicates, keeping community votes meaningful. Municipalities can push announcements directly to residents. Built entirely in Figma Make. No external editors, no context switching, first prompt to finished app, all in Fi"

author: Shajith Ahamed
body: "We ask AI questions every day. But what if AI could do more than generate answers? What if it could bring the right people together, organize diverse perspectives, and help uncover better solutions? That question led me to build PerspectiveX. Unlike traditional social platforms where valuable ideas often get buried in endless feeds and comment threads, PerspectiveX transforms every challenge into a structured collaboration. People contribute diverse perspectives, AI helps organize and analyze them, and the strongest ideas naturally rise to the top. At the heart of the experience is Prism AI—po"

author: Jithin Roy
body:
"What if a design community felt like walking into a tiny multiplayer world?
Meet Lounge. 🛋️✨
I built a live web space where design friends can hang out, explore, and share their work together.
I drew the map, art, and logo in Figma, then brought it to life with Figma Make and Supabase MCP.
Every guest gets a spot on the map: pick a fox or a rabbit, choose your color, and walk right in.
The fun part is the zones. Step into the green zone to show your art, say hello at a friend in the orange zone, and move around the space like you’re actually there.
I wanted Lounge to feel less like a page you"

author: John Freund
body: "Link: Working File-  https://www.figma.com/make/hymnP9Wz7O4nMy1eivvYra/Create-storyboard?t=kYXgdkHvFol6Sgd4-1 Link Live File link-  https://www.figma.com/make/hymnP9Wz7O4nMy1eivvYra/Create-storyboard?t=kYXgdkHvFol6Sgd4-20&fullscreen=1 HOMEIQ is an AI-powered home repair assessment app that helps homeowners instantly determine whether damage is a safe DIY fix or requires urgent professional help. Built entirely with Figma Make, the project includes a full prototype storyboard, camera scan flow, AI analysis screen, dual-outcome results, and a brand standards guide — all in one working file"

author: Maria Alejandra Guerrero
body:
"Hey, I'm Ale! ClearPath is an AI-powered understanding assistant that transforms complex public information into personalized, actionable guidance. By adapting explanations to a person's context, goals, literacy level and learning preferences, ClearPath helps people navigate benefits, public services, policies and administrative processes with clarity and confidence. Because the real question isn't ""What does this document say?"" It's ""What does this mean for me?""  https://www.loom.com/share/f2a98dc5565d40949ca27a861690f70c

author: Rhea Hinger
body:
"Hand Puppet Gallery — a fun, vibe-coded exploration built with Figma Make.

""Design matters more than ever"" — but matters for what? Most design today is built to capture us: to hold our attention, drive clicks, optimize our behavior. Hand Puppet Gallery is a small, hopeful argument for the other kind — design that hands something back. No skill, no metric, no goal. Just imagination, and your own two hands, and the oldest kind of play there is. Because the design that matters most isn't the kind that keeps us staring — it's the kind that makes us feel awake, curious, alive again.

To Interact: "

author: Kate Ilias

Then I go and I design something I can actually touch. That is exactly how I got passionate about jewelry making and ended up starting my own little atelier. Working with raw materials, textures, and shapes always gives me completely new energy. But then, an idea sparked: How about transforming this experience back to the digital space? What if we could bring that tactile, raw freedom of physical cr"

body:

SignMate is an experimental sign language learning experience designed to make communication more inclusive. Check here for live link -  SignMate  ❤️ Inspired by my personal experiences communicating with family members who have hearing and speech impairments, SignMate explores how AI, computer vision, and accessibility-focused design can help more people learn sign language through practice and real-time feedback. Users can learn signs from AI-generated tutor demonstrations, practice them using their webcam, and receive instant v"

author: Faadil Boussari
body: "Your strongest work is often the work you cannot show. CaseCraft is an AI-assisted portfolio tool that helps professionals turn confidential project work into public proof. It reviews each claim, classifies it as SAFE, ANONYMIZE, or EXCLUDE, and lets the user review or change every decision before generating a privacy-safe case study. Built with Figma Make, Claude API, Cloudflare Workers, and JavaScript, CaseCraft explores a simple question: how can professionals prove the value of their best work without exposing private information? Live project:  https://rank-cheer-67549692.figma.site"

author: Qi Rifky
body: "I built Contestify a tool to help creators discover online contests and competitions from various platforms and social media, manage projects, track wins, and build a career portfolio, all in one place. As someone who has been active in the contest-hunting community for a long time, I know that there are countless online contests and competitions held every single day,design contests, hackathons, meme contests, and writing competitions. I always felt there was one big problem that nobody ever thought about.We join dozens of contests, but we don't know how many times we've won or lost, everythi"

author: ONE Studioz
body:
"Project Name: LegalLocale ""Zoning laws, decoded for the people who live there"". 
Maria wanted a community garden. The city sent her 200 pages of legal jargon. She couldn't tell if a garden was allowed. LegalLocale fixes this. Upload a zoning PDF. MCP parses it into structured rules. Make generates a visual map — height limits, setbacks, permitted uses — all spatial, all clear. Agent organizes it into a canvas neighbors can comment on. Zoning shouldn't require a law degree. 
Problem: 
Zoning codes determine what gets built in every neighborhood. But they're written in dense legal language, lock"

author: Sarmad Mustafa
body: "1.38 million people. That's how many were affected when a magnitude 7.8 earthquake hit Mindanao, Philippines on June 8. 68 dead. 70,000 displaced. Hospitals evacuating their own patients. Networks down, phones dark. When I was young, In 2005 our 7.6 quake killed over 80,000 people and left 2.8 million homeless. 19,000 of them were children, buried under their own schools. I grew up knowing that in a disaster, the people we lose first are usually the ones who couldn't get help in time. And in those first minutes, just knowing costs time that you don't have. What's their blood type? Do they have"

author: Federica De Sio
body: "What if you couldn’t trust your own memory? Dream Museum is an interactive experience about how memory shifts, fragments, and quietly changes over time. As you move through it, certainty breaks down—meanings blur, time feels unstable, and spaces subtly respond to your attention. It’s not about simulating cognitive conditions, but about the feeling of fragile continuity we all experience in different ways. Built with Figma Make, I explored interaction, motion, and behavior as storytelling tools, not just screens. At the end, you leave a memory fragment that slowly fades away. Would love to hear"

author: Sebastian Lumbi
body: "At this Makeathon, I wanted to solve a personal frustration I face when working with typography digitally. My home setup is centered around a drawing tablet, and I really dislike using a mouse when I am trying to be creative. Standard digital typography tools require a lot of clicking, and you are forced to know exactly what every panel, slider, value, and term does. To solve this, I created 'Text Sculptor.' It brings the tactile feel of digital sculpting brushes to typography. With this tool, you don't need to worry about technical concepts like 'font weight,' 'letter spacing,' or 'line spaci"

author: muhammad najeeb
body: "APEX MOTORS A cinematic luxury automotive experience built for enthusiasts who expect more than a gallery. APEX MOTORS reimagines how iconic automobiles are presented online, transforming a traditional showcase into an immersive digital exhibition. Designed with a dark editorial aesthetic, the experience blends ultra-premium visual storytelling, dramatic motion, and high-performance interactions to create the feeling of walking through a private luxury car collection. The project features legendary marques including Rolls-Royce, Lamborghini, Ferrari, BMW, Porsche, and Bentley, each presented t"

An AR-powered mobile experience that turns a simple walk in the park into an educational journey. By scanning trees, users get instant access to historical data, health status, and ecological impact of our urban giants. Key features:
✅ AR-based tree detection & scanning.
✅ Historical data (Dendrochronology).
✅ Carbon sequestration & vitality tracking. This project was a deep dive into ""building in public,"" iterating on des"

author: Oliver Engel

body:
"Curvature Project link
Link to Curvature
 https://www.figma.com/make/cXF6ey0n5jDt3MC1GoY5vq/Curvature?t=9B96hf3YeJKWheDY-1
 This computer vision tool called Curvature Extractor & Piecewise Reconstructor, developed entirely within the Figma Make ecosystem. The application allows you to upload an image, draw a freehand polygon over an object, and mathematically extract and model the curves and edges inside that area. Every detected segment is fitted with a parametric polynomial equation. The reconstruction canvas in the Figma environment supports zooming, panning, and hovering over segment label"

body: "Hi all, I’m happy to submit my entry for the Config Makeathon.  Problem: Users struggle to follow cooking videos efficiently while cooking. They often lose control of pacing, get distracted, or realize the recipe doesn’t match the ingredients they already have at home. Solution: I designed a cooking experience where users stay fully in control of the cooking process. Instead of passive video consumption, the app breaks recipes into interactive steps. Users can: > Move through steps manually or using voice control > Filter recipes based on available ingredients at home > Save favorite recipes f"

author: Mingzhe Fu
body:

It creates a flavor bridge through foods they already know, so restaurants can explain new dishes in a clearer and more welcoming way. author: Kateryna Tonofrei
body: "We rewrite short messages obsessively. ""Thanks."" or ""Thanks!"" or ""Thank you so much!!"" Too cold, too eager, too much. A tiny daily anxiety nobody designs for. Every writing tool answers the wrong question. They tell you what tone you wrote: friendly, professional, concise. None tell you how the person reading it will actually receive it. Noted is a little machine for exactly that. Type the message you're rehearsing, then turn a dial from too cold to too warm. Your message morphs live as you go, and underneath it you see the other side of the conversation: what the reader is probably thinking a"

author: Svetlana Nešković
body: "Reality Check 🚀  Reality Check started with a question I kept asking myself while experimenting with AI design tools: Can a real human actually survive this experience? Today, AI can generate screens, assets, prototypes, and even complete user flows in seconds. But there is still one thing it doesn't tell us: how real people might experience what we create. To explore that idea, I used ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini for research, brainstorming, UX exploration, and prompt development. I then used Figma Make to transform those ideas into a working product. My prediction is that Figma's next AI cha"

author: Diana Dudnyk
body: "Most people don't struggle with setting goals. They struggle with staying consistent. That's why I created Hatchly — a habit tracker where every habit becomes a dragon that grows alongside you. Each new habit starts as an egg. As you complete your habits day after day, your dragon grows and evolves with you. Miss a day, and your dragon falls asleep, reminding you to come back and continue your journey. Along the way, you'll discover and collect unique dragons. Some are common, while others are much rarer. Can you unlock them all? And if you're lucky enough, maybe you'll discover Lumi — the rar"

author: Adesh Chaudhary
body: "My mom once sat in a doctor's office for 3 minutes after waiting 3 weeks for the appointment. She had been tracking everything in her head. The headaches. The fatigue. When it got worse. What made it better. The doctor asked how she was feeling. She said ""not great."" That was it. That was all she could say in the moment. I built HEARD because that conversation deserved better. You log how you feel every day, plain language, and the AI finds what you cannot articulate when someone is staring at you across a desk. Built for the Figma Config Makeathon. Final submission today. BE — Heard."

author: Sam A.
body:
"Hello creatives! Here's my submission for the #Config Makeathon 🙌 

Idea: Close your eyes. Draw a circle. Don't correct it. That's the entire interaction behind ENSŌ, built for Figma's Config Makeathon 2026. The idea borrows from a Zen practice called ensō, a single brushstroke circle drawn in one breath and never corrected. The imperfection isn't the failure. It's the point. The app asks you to draw blind. An AI reads the shape your hand made, the gap you left open, the direction you moved, where your energy dropped mid-stroke, and returns a short reading of whatever you were holding onto in"

author: Mariana Miguel
body:
"Created  Whirly  for Figma's Makeathon! ➿🔊

You record a sound, any sound, a voice, a hum, something nonsensical, and it becomes a Whirl. Other people can explore and layer their own sound on top, and it grows into a living spiral of everyone's sounds. It's the Whirl Wide Web after all!

The process was a lot of fun because it became a constant exercise in imagining how this strange thing could work. Defining the rules, structuring the logic, designing the interactions, building the visual language, and figuring out how sound could behave as something people collectively shape over time.

Eve"

author: Rishi Shah
body:
"I watched  Apple's 108-second event  recap and couldn't stop thinking about one thing. No voiceover. No presenter. Just words, motion, and rhythm. Short sentences. Each one a beat. The entire year of Apple products - described in 108 seconds through kinetic typography alone. That moment became TypeDeck. The idea: What if After Effects had a baby with PowerPoint - and it was actually simple enough for anyone to use in minutes? No timeline. No keyframes. No layers. Just write your script and watch it become a film.

I Built the entire app UI and animation engine from prompt to code. The behavior"

author: Ankur chandra
body:
"BEFORE YOU SIGN 
You sign contracts written by someone with a lawyer. You sign them without one. 🖊️ THE PROBLEM
We've all had the moment keys in your hand, an empty apartment behind you, and a lease slid across the table to sign. Or a contract lands in your inbox and you sign it just to get the project started. Nobody reads the buried terms; you check the rent or the pay and move on. I learned the hard way. I didn't get my security deposit back from one rental, and another time a clause meant I got paid less than I was promised. The catch: every one of those was written by someone with a lawy"

Built with Figma Make

body: "I built a logic gate puzzle game. Here's why it matters for AI. Everyone in 2026 is talking about prompt engineering, large language models, and neural networks. But beneath the surface of every single AI breakthrough lies a simple, beautiful reality: billions of tiny switches turning ON and OFF. To truly understand AI, we need to understand Boolean logic. That’s why I built Flumine for the @Figma #ConfigMakeathon. 🕹️ Flumine is a minimalist, neon-infused puzzle game that takes us on a 30-level journey from a single logic switch to complex digital circuits. No boring textbooks, no dry code. J"

author: Ashwin Goyal
body:
"There are thousands of AI apps that help you make decisions. I wanted to build one that refuses to. Introducing LIVING WITH IT. https://livingwithit.figma.site/   Most of us don't need more advice. We already know what we want to do—we're trapped in the endless cycle of replaying the same decision, searching for permission to choose differently. LIVING WITH IT breaks that loop. You make one irreversible decision.
You write a letter to your future self.
Then the decision is sealed. From that point on, the app refuses to let you re-litigate your choice. Instead, your future self writes back ever"

author: SAHANA SOOD
body:
"After loved ones pass away, Some people live in your phone.
In a voicemail you haven't deleted.
In the way they texted - always with a typo, always with love. I built something for them. ✦ Heirloom ✦ A place where you keep the people you carry with you.
Not an app. Not a memorial. Just somewhere quiet to put what you still have of them. You add their name. You bring what you have - a photo, a voice note, a screenshot. Heirloom does the rest.
Their words come back to you.
A different memory every time you open it. Private. Yours. Always there.

Grief doesn’t ask for much.
Just somewhere to put "

author: Tanya Zabolotna
body:
"Idea:
A gesture-played duet instrument where a second voice yields when you play, enters when you rest, and cuts itself off the instant you re-engage. Both go quiet, the canvas freezes, you keep the image. Problem and how I solved it:
AI collaboration means the AI decides when to act. You participate but don't author the rules. I treated AI behavior the way design systems treat components: three variables, Initiative, Yielding, Mirroring, define how the second voice behaves. Why it matters:
Everyone's worried about AI taking over. The answer is a framework where you author the rules and Interp"

author: Jade Sans
body: "Phrame : Figma slides to real, editable Powerpoint templates.  Hand off a Powerpoint template to your clients without spending hours rebuilding it twice. A PowerPoint template (.potx) isn't just a slide deck. It defines the reusable layouts themselves: the placeholder positions, fonts, and colors that every new slide inherits when you click ""New Slide."" Most Figma-to-PowerPoint tools just export flat images or static text boxes in a pptx file. Phrame builds the actual master template structure. Paste a Figma file URL, pick a page, review the slides and get a downloadable .potx with working Sli"

body: "Iterations — every design version remembers why it changed. Here's something every designer quietly deals with. You make a design. People give feedback. You change it. You change it again. And again. By version 5, nobody remembers why version 2 got thrown out or what that one good comment on version 3 actually said. The reasons just… disappear. AI design tools made this worse, not better. They save every version, sure — but you can only ever look at one at a time. Want to see how today's design compares to last week's? author: lotfi harbaoui
body: "Naqsh (نقش) is a living mosaic wall the world builds together. Place a ceramic tile on an authentic 8-fold Islamic geometry grid, and watch it appear in real time on every visitor's screen  then rise into a 3D courtyard with ambient oud and a craftsman's voice. Every tile is permanent. The wall remembers every hand that touched it. Built solo in Tunis. How it was built: I wrote the zellige geometry as pure mathematics and injected it into Figma Make, wired Supabase realtime for live multiplayer, and used Three.js for procedural 3D extrusion + ElevenLabs for the audio layer.   Stack: Figma Make"

author: Sandel  Concepcion
body:
"Most productivity tools assume you can look at a list of 40 things and just pick one. That's not how ADHD works. NeuroLaunch OS shows you one glowing tile. You click it. You do it. You move to the next one. It's a goal-setting and execution system built around one constraint: show the user only what they need right now. You answer 3 questions — project, work type, timeline — and the app builds itself: starter goals, a 5-phase roadmap, a curated resource library, all generated for your context. Then you move through 6 sequential islands:

Start → Goals → Focus → Roadmap → Resources → Pitch One "

"I built Somna to help people to fall asleep ✨

Many people struggle to fall asleep because they are trying too hard to sleep. The more attention they give to sleep, the more awake they become.

Most sleep apps focus on meditation, tracking, scores, streaks, or productivity. Somna takes a different approach. Instead of helping people sleep, it helps them stop trying.

Users choose what's keeping them awake tonight, and Somna guides them into a calming activity designed for that feeling—whether they're overthinking, anxious, restless, or simply exhausted after a long day.

Built with Figma Make,"

author: George Balinov
body:
"Critique is the most valuable thing in design and the hardest to get fast. So for the Figma Config Makeathon I built Crit-Crit! Train your design eye by spotting issues on real websites — vague headlines, competing CTAs, failing contrast — scored against expert consensus. Miss something? It teaches you why. The part that makes it more than a game: the sites you train on are real. Your best pins become real feedback for the builder who shipped it. Give critique → get better → get critique back. One loop. 

Built end-to-end in @figma Make."

author: Sagar Singh
body: "My grandmother has stories I've never asked her to tell. So I'm building Dhaaga (धागा : ""thread"") for the Figma Config Makeathon. It's simple: I ask her one question. She talks. The app listens in Hindi, keeps her words exactly as she said them, and translates them for me. The story becomes a little glowing star. Add more stars and they form a constellation of everyone in our family who has ever spoken. The AI also notices which years of her life have no stars yet — and gently suggests what to ask next. No streaks. No feed. Just her voice, kept. Link:  https://www.figma.com/make/kM6cJtVxUCSiwg"

What if a plant-care game could feel calm, expressive, and a little magical? In the game, each plant reacts through mood changes, care meters, facial expressions, and small interactions. Users can water the plant, give it sunlight, watch it grow, and eventually discover hybrid plants like Glowleaf, Honeybloom, and Dreamberry. This project was also special for me because it was my first"

author: Lisa Dion
body: "CommonZone: Transform vacant commercial buildings into vibrant, affordable communities with an interactive platform that visualizes potential renovations and engages users. The US is short more than 4 million homes, while empty malls and offices sit idle across the country. The space already exists. We just tear it down instead of rethinking it. Pick a real vacant building, like Lakeside Mall or the Free Press building, design a community on its actual footprint, and watch the impact add up live: homes, jobs, emissions cut, cost. Everything came out bolder than I designed it. My first thought was ""I missed a weight."" But I checked — I'd loaded every Satoshi weight, italics included. I did everything right. author: Dian Saputra
body: "KeyDash  is a web-based typing game built for the Config Makeathon using Figma Make. The idea came from a simple problem: typing practice is useful, but it can feel repetitive and not very motivating. I wanted to explore how a clean typing-test interface could be combined with a playful game mechanic. In  KeyDash , players type random English words while a pixel-art runner tries to stay ahead of a human chaser. Every correct word increases the lead, while mistakes and slow typing make the chaser get closer. Players can choose difficulty, time duration, character, and map before starting a run."

author: Ashley Phillips
body: "After a bunch of research and prep, the core engine of CookieDraft is calculating correctly. Icing totals update in real time and match exactly what the user draws. No visual design pass yet, only layout and foundations. I’ll probably say that ten more times because the hardest part of a two-week deadline is stopping yourself from adding effects and gradients early. All the prep really paid off: 100+ components, tight auto-layout hierarchy, React-style naming, a published library, and clear markdown specs. I was so nervous about hitting that first build button for no reason. I use code-based d"

Which means the moment the curtains open—triggered by detecting your hand—the browser silently blocks any audio that tries to play. No warning. No error. Just silence. I tried everything. Different audio sources. Different playback methods. Different browser APIs. At one point, I managed to g"

body:
"I built a 2000-year-old Tamil board game using Figma Make for the Config Makeathon 2026. Growing up, every summer meant sitting beside my grandmother playing Pallanguzhi — a traditional Tamil strategy game. As an adult, I realized many people my age had stopped playing it. The boards were still there. The memories were still there. But somewhere along the way, we forgot to pick up the seeds. So I rebuilt it. ✨ Full game logic — sowing, captures, Muthu, Thakkam, series mode
👵 AI opponent ""Paatti"" with personality, commentary and Tamil wisdom

body: "I spent a lot of time today working on the game-building phase in Figma Make. Honestly, there were so many iterations and errors, especially since I’m a bit of a perfectionist when it comes to UI/UX design, that I didn’t realize how many tokens I was using. However, I learned a few things to save Figma Make tokens: - List the issues you’ll fix, then list them and have the agent create a plan (I even managed to create 9 questions at once), that’s more efficient than fixing them one by one - Clear out excessive context, because AI is more efficient if it doesn’t have to read through previous con"

The nurse needs to know where it hurts and how badly, and right now that conversation happens through pointing, Google Translate, or a cousin on speakerphone. Babel is a tablet you hand the patient: they tap where it hurts, pick how the pain feels, choose a face for the severity, and it builds a card the clinician can read or hear in their own language. author: Eugene Riabov
body:
"As a web designer, I get better by paying attention to great websites - the layouts, the type, the little details that make me think ""I want to be that good.""

Collecting them is how I build my taste. But there's a catch nobody talks about: websites don't last. The site that inspires you today can be a 404 tomorrow. 

Redesigns, dead domains, projects quietly taken offline - and just like that, the design is gone. You didn't just lose a bookmark. You lost the reference, and a little piece of what was shaping your work. 

So I built SiteSnap - a tool to collect website designs as inspiration, s"

author: Sai Kumar
body: "Endo Advocate — a personalized appointment prep tool for women with endometriosis Recently I've been thinking about how to use my design skills for meaningful impact (and watching too much Grey's Anatomy). So when I saw the prompt: solve a problem, I picked one that affects 1 in 10 women: endometriosis. It takes 7 to 11 years on average to diagnose, partly because symptoms get dismissed in the exam room, and partly because until recently it could only be confirmed through surgery. ACOG's 2026 guidelines changed that, allowing a clinical diagnosis from symptoms alone. What I built: Endo Advocat"

author: Bartu Alankaya
body: "I joined this year's Config Makeathon and decided to build something inspired by a problem I've repeatedly encountered while working in CRM and lifecycle marketing. CRM platforms are built around flows, campaigns, and automations. Customers are not. When trying to understand what a customer actually experienced, I often found myself jumping between multiple journeys, templates, campaigns, and reports just to answer a simple question: What happened, why did it happen, and what happens next? So I built Trace. Trace is a lifecycle debugging tool that helps teams: • Replay customer journeys in a s"

author: Sovan E
body:
"Hey everyone! 

I made Meadow🙌  — a small, shared corner to step out of the world for a minute.

Some days the world is just a lot. Meadow is a small, shared corner to step out of it for a minute. It's a living garden grown by strangers — every flower in it was built, petal by petal, by a real person somewhere, and the field keeps growing with each one. Make your own bloom from scratch: shape the petals, choose the colors, set its height, give the stem its own curve and leaves. Plant it, and it joins the meadow for the next person to find. Flowers growing close together slowly cross-pollinate"

author: Vrutika Chavan
body: "For my Figma Makeathon submission, I built  The Postcard , a web app that transforms ordinary messages into personalized digital postcards. Users can customize their postcard with fonts, colors, stamps, and photos, then instantly generate a unique link to share with friends and family. The goal was to make digital communication feel more expressive while keeping the experience simple and accessible. Built using Figma Make, the project explores how customization and thoughtful interaction design can turn a basic message into something worth sharing. ✨ Personalize it. 🔗 Share it. 💌 Deliver it."

author: Abrham Kinfegebreal
body: "Project name: Figma Signal Description: Every great product starts with research. The problem? That research happens outside Figma forty-seven browser tabs, scattered opinions, no clear direction. By the time people open Figma, they've often already decided to build the wrong thing. Figma Signal fixes that. Type your idea and it scans real communities (Reddit, G2, ProductHunt) for real signals pain points, demand, opportunity. Then it generates a validated product concept and hands you wireframes ready for Figma Make. The flow: Signal Discovery → Pain Analysis → Opportunity Detection → Product"

author: Apoorva Kalasannavar
body: "🧠 Finding My Idea for the #configmakeathon Joining the Config Makeathon has been exciting—and unexpectedly difficult. The funny thing is, I'm usually an ""idea girl."" I get a new project idea every bathroom break. But this time, the challenge wasn't finding an idea. It was figuring out which idea was worth building. After days of thinking, I stopped asking: ""What problem should I solve?"" and started asking: ""If software is no longer just a functional tool, but a limitless canvas for human expression, what is the one feeling I am uniquely equipped to digitize?"" That question changed everything."

body: "DesignDNA : GitHub for Design Thinking GitHub tracks code history. DesignDNA tracks design thinking. The Problem Design teams are great at documenting what changed. Developers receive specifications without understanding intent. Over time, products slowly drift away from the principles that originally shaped them. "What if learning design felt like playing Duolingo?
 I noticed that most design resources feel like textbooks — dense, passive, and easy to abandon. So I built something different.
Meet Hue — a gamified design education app where your mascot reacts to your answers, a placement test finds your level, and you can scan any real design for instant feedback.
The mascot was important to me. It changes color based on how you're doing — nervous when you haven't answered, sad when you're wrong, celebrating when you get it right. Small detail, big difference in how learning feels.
Other features include"

author: Shadow Kayn
body:
"1 in 4 people globally struggle with their mental health. Most wellness apps feel clinical, overwhelming, or just too much to open on a hard day. So for the #ConfigMakeathon, I built Check In — a 3-screen mental health check-in app designed to feel human, not clinical. Here's how it works:
→ Pick your mood (no account, no tracking, no pressure)
→ Get a gentle journal prompt matched to how you're feeling
→ Receive one science-backed grounding tool — box breathing, 5-4-3-2-1, or a small nudge The whole thing resets with one button. That's it. Simple on purpose. Built in using @Figma Make — desig"

author: Adesh Chaudhary
body: opened a blank doc this morning for the Config Makeathon. wrote down every idea that came to mind. then started crossing them out. too small. too obvious. already exists. nobody cares. got to one that made me stop. it's a problem hundreds of millions of people deal with. every single day. and nobody has ever built something beautiful for it. not once. spent the rest of today figuring out how it should feel before i write a single line of code. warm. quiet. editorial. the opposite of what every other app in this space looks like. no screens yet. just a direction. 13 days left.

author: Talia Przybyszewski
body:
"Day 1: The Idea Started with a question on Reddit: what places do you still think about that are gone? Over 200 people replied across London, Liverpool and New York in under an hour. ""The city lost a piece of its soul when it closed.""
""I still think about it when I get back from the gym.""
""I wonder if I imagined it sometimes."" That was the brief. Last Seen is an archive of lost places and the ones still worth finding. Three cities. Real memories from real people. A map that shows what we've lost and what's still here. Built tonight: Supabase schema, 49 seeded places with real memories, live on"

author: Suyash Tambe
body:
"VisorHUD is a helmet heads-up display concept for motorcycle riders, built under the Halox brand. The problem: Riders in India rely on phone mounts for navigation. At 80 km/h, a two second glance at a phone means 44 metres travelled blind. With 200 million two-wheeler riders in India alone, this is a real and daily safety risk. The solution: A HUD display that sits between the rider's face and the helmet visor, projecting speed, navigation, blind spot warnings, and fuel level directly at eye level through the Halox companion app. What was designed:

body: "🚀 YouTube Creator Dashboard I'm excited to share my project, YouTube Creator Dashboard, built for the Figma Config Makeathon. author: Tannita Digpati
body:
"PROJECT TITLE: QuickHelp - Crisis Support Finder

PROJECT DESCRIPTION:
QuickHelp is a web app that helps people find mental health resources instantly. When someone is in crisis, they shouldn't have to search multiple sites or apps they need help fast.  Built with Figma Make for AI-powered code generation and Supabase for real resource data, QuickHelp auto-detects your location and displays verified crisis hotlines, therapists, and support services in seconds. Designed to remove barriers between people in need and the support that can save their lives.

Demo link:  https://review-coat-77569143"

author: Beatriz Catarino Martins
body: "Me and Juli wanted to explore how we could mix two things we love in games: the deduction feeling of Murdoku and the live, chaotic, social drawing energy of Scribblio. That became Where’s the Pup? - a cozy multiplayer drawing mystery where players join through a shared link, get one private clue, draw one at a time, and then use everyone’s drawings to guess which room the pup is hiding in. We built it with Figma Make + Supabase: Figma Make powers the interactive game flow, while Supabase manages the challenge system, clue cards, answer rooms, and main vs. extra clues for different player count"

author: Mcnove Pineda
body: "Between Us Between Us is an emotionally immersive shared digital space designed to help people feel emotionally closer in a world where communication often feels fast, noisy, and emotionally disconnected. Instead of building another traditional messaging or social platform, this project explores how atmosphere, cinematic storytelling, emotional presence, and immersive interaction design can create deeper and more meaningful digital connection between two people. The experience introduces emotionally adaptive atmosphere systems: Memory Mode Presence Mode Dream Mode Healing Mode Celebration Mode"

author: Lukáš Caletka
body:
"TrustFlow — AI Safety & Escrow Layer for Online Marketplaces This is my submission for the Config Makeathon. I created TrustFlow as an AI-powered safety and escrow layer for online marketplaces — helping detect risky listings, verify sellers, protect payments, warn users during suspicious chats, and support dispute resolution before fraud causes real harm. The full project case study, including the required video, is published here:
 https://contra.com/p/6vUAoFr2-trust-flow-ai-safety-and-escrow-layer-for-online-marketplaces Published Figma prototype:
 https://radio-ranch-95224118.figma.site"

author: Lukáš Caletka
body: "TrustFlow — AI Safety & Escrow Layer for Online Marketplaces Online marketplaces are powerful, but trust often breaks at the exact moment it matters most: when money, identity, communication, and delivery all meet in one transaction. TrustFlow is an AI-powered safety and escrow layer designed to protect buyers, sellers, and marketplace platforms before fraud happens. The product combines: • AI listing risk analysis • seller verification and reputation signals • protected escrow checkout • scam-warning chat detection • automatic dispute support • admin review queues for suspicious cases I used "
